pi_items.source: Wikipedia, Cancer Research UK, Mayo Clinic, FDA drug databases - Nilotinib is a BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase inhibitor used to treat chronic myelogenous leukemia (CML) with Philadelphia chromosome
CDC, NIH, StatPearls - Chloroquine is a confirmed antimalarial drug used for malaria prophylaxis, though chloroquine-resistant Plasmodium spp. are now widespread
CDC, NIH, StatPearls - Mefloquine is a confirmed antimalarial drug targeting blood schizonts, effective against chloroquine-resistant P. falciparum
CDC, NIH, StatPearls - Doxycycline is a confirmed antimalarial drug targeting blood schizonts, one of the most commonly prescribed prophylactic agents
WebMD, Drugs.com, HelloPharmacist, Medscape - Chloroquine increases QTc-prolonging activities of Nilotinib, causing serious cardiac interaction requiring caution or avoidance
Medscape, NIH, medical references - Mefloquine may increase QTc-prolonging activities of Nilotinib, classified as serious interaction requiring avoidance or close monitoring with ECG surveillance

Cancer Drug Interactions (University of Liverpool/Radboud UMC), medical references - Doxycycline undergoes no significant metabolism and is excreted intact renally/bilially. Coadministration with Nilotinib has not been studied but based on metabolism and clearance, a clinically significant interaction is unlikely (Green = no interaction expected)

Drug interaction pharmacology and clinical guidelines - Doxycycline is the safest choice for malaria prophylaxis in a patient taking Nilotinib because: (1) Chloroquine and Mefloquine both prolong QT interval like Nilotinib (serious interaction), (2) Doxycycline has no clinically significant interaction with Nilotinib, (3) Studies show doxycycline has fewer side effects than alternatives
Task setup scripts (setup_task.sh and export_result.sh) - Path is consistent with Android task environment

privileged_info_summary: Nilotinib is a real BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase inhibitor cancer drug used to treat chronic myelogenous leukemia. The three malaria prophylaxis options (Chloroquine, Mefloquine, Doxycycline) are all real antimalarial drugs. Chloroquine and Mefloquine both prolong the QT interval and create serious drug-drug interactions with Nilotinib (Red/Orange traffic light colors indicating serious interactions requiring caution or avoidance). Doxycycline undergoes no significant metabolism and is excreted intact renally, creating no clinically significant interaction with Nilotinib (Green traffic light color). Therefore, Doxycycline is the correct and safest recommendation for malaria prophylaxis in a patient taking Nilotinib. This assessment aligns with the University of Liverpool Cancer Drug Interactions database methodology and clinical pharmacology principles.
